architecture.summary: AdapTrack is a constrained decoding method for autoregressive language models that incorporates adaptive backtracking to preserve the model's output intent while satisfying hard constraints. It maintains per-prefix validity estimates and samples for each generated token, and uses rejection sampling to dynamically backtrack when the proportion of invalid continuations is large, allowing the model to explore alternative token sequences that better align with the underlying language model distribution while respecting constraints.

biblio.title: AdapTrack: Constrained Decoding without Distorting LLM's Output Intent

claims.dataset: TFv1 (DeepSeek Coder Base 6.7B, TensorFlow v2 setting, EM@1)
claims.statement: AdapTrack can achieve up to 360.87% improvement compared to constrained decoding on the synthetic API completion dataset (TFv1)
AdapTrack can achieve up to 38.93% improvement over constrained decoding on the real-world API completion dataset (TFv1Real)
AdapTrack achieves up to 7.84% improvement on HumanEval pass@1 compared to constrained decoding

claims.dataset: MBPP (DeepSeek Coder Base 6.7B, TypeScript via MultiPL-E)
claims.statement: AdapTrack achieves up to 6.42% improvement on MBPP pass@1 compared to constrained decoding

The distribution produced by AdapTrack aligns with the model's distribution given the generated tokens, satisfying the property that for any two valid complete sequences, their probability ratio under AdapTrack equals their probability ratio under the language model

Experiments on domain-specific language problems show that the distribution produced by AdapTrack has significantly lower KL divergence from the correct distribution compared to existing methods, indicating closer alignment with the actual model distribution
AdapTrack sometimes performs worse than unconstrained decoding on pass@1 metrics (e.g., StarCoder2 7B and CodeLlama 7B on MBPP), attributed to constrainer limitations

Constrained decoding distorts the model's output intent by forcing the model to generate code that satisfies constraints but deviates from the model's expected functionality and contextual semantics, as exemplified by the PyTorch matrix_rank case where constrained decoding produces matrix_power instead of linalg.matrix_rank
